title: Chocolate Swiss roll
subtitle: A thin chocolate sponge rolled around whipped cream
excerpt: A flat chocolate sponge baked quickly in a Swiss roll tin, turned out onto sugared paper and rolled while still warm to set the curve, then unrolled and filled with whipped cream.
sourceNotes: The Swiss roll as a technique is documented in English baking from the mid-nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, gives a plain rolled sponge with jam. The chocolate sponge variant using cocoa powder in a fatless génoise batter became widespread in British home baking in the early twentieth century, documented in Florence White, Good Things in England (1932).

content.text: baked thin, then rolled around a filling before it cools. The rolling step is the part that worries people: it must be done while the sponge is still warm enough to bend without cracking. The technique is to turn it out onto a sheet of baking paper dusted with caster sugar, roll it up while empty (this sets the muscle memory of the curve), leave it to cool, then unroll, fill, and re-roll. Cracking on the second roll is cosmetic and easily disguised with a dusting of icing sugar.

The sponge is cocoa-flavoured and baked for only 10 to 12 minutes. It should be springy but still soft. Over-baking makes it dry and brittle, which is when it actually cracks badly. The filling here is whipped double cream, plain or lightly sweetened. Raspberry jam spread thinly under the cream is the traditional addition.

attrs.prepNote: for dusting and lightly sweetening cream

content.text: Heat the oven to 190°C fan (210°C conventional). Line a 33 × 23 cm Swiss roll tin with baking paper. Put {{eggs}} and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl or the bowl of a stand mixer. Whisk on high speed for about six minutes until the mixture is very pale, thick, and has tripled in volume. When you lift the whisk, the ribbon of batter falling back should hold its shape for three seconds before dissolving. Sift the {{plain-flour}} and {{cocoa-powder}} together over the batter and fold in gently with a large metal spoon using as few strokes as possible. Work from the bottom up and cut through the mixture rather than stirring.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin and spread it level, into the corners. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes. The sponge is ready when the surface springs back when pressed lightly and has just pulled away from the edges. Do not overbake.

Lay a sheet of baking paper on a work surface and dust it generously with caster sugar. Turn the hot sponge out onto the paper and peel away the lining paper. Working from a short end, tightly roll the sponge up inside the sugared paper. Leave rolled for 30 minutes until completely cool.

Whip {{double-cream}} with half the {{icing-sugar}} to soft peaks. Unroll the sponge carefully. Spread the cream evenly over the surface, leaving a 2 cm border at the far edge. Re-roll the sponge away from you, using the paper to help. Place seam-side down on a serving plate and dust the top with the remaining {{icing-sugar}}.
Surface cracks on re-rolling are normal and cosmetic. A thick dusting of icing sugar hides all but the deepest cracks.
Room-temperature eggs whisk to a much greater volume than cold eggs. If yours are from the fridge, sit them in warm water for 10 minutes before whisking.
For a bûche de Noël variation: spread the outside with chocolate ganache and rake a fork along it to mimic bark.
